does: This paper introduces SafeClawBench, a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing security vulnerabilities in tool-using language model agents. The benchmark contains 600 adversarial tasks organized across six attack families: direct and indirect prompt injection, tool-return injection, memory poisoning, memory extraction, and ambiguity-driven unsafe inference. The key innovation is decomposing attack outcomes into three distinct measurement stages—semantic attack acceptance (whether the model agrees with malicious instructions), audit-visible harm evidence (detectable traces in logs or outputs), and sandbox-observed tool/state harm (actual effects on systems or data). The authors evaluate five agent implementations against four prompt-level defense policies, revealing that semantic failure rates range from 9.0% to 44.2% across models, and critically, that these three endpoints capture fundamentally different failure modes that a single success metric would obscure.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that tool-use introduces qualitatively different safety challenges beyond traditional unsafe text generation. It extends prior work on prompt injection attacks, jailbreaking, and agent robustness by providing the first systematic staged framework that distinguishes between what an agent says, what auditable evidence shows, and what actually happens in deployed systems. The benchmark responds to limitations in existing agent evaluation methodologies that conflate semantic compliance with actual harm, drawing on traditions from security research that emphasize threat modeling and precise vulnerability categorization. It engages with concurrent work on agent security, memory-based attacks on language models, and the intersection of LLM alignment and concrete system effects.
enables: SafeClawBench establishes a foundation for developing more granular defenses tailored to specific failure points in the agent pipeline rather than monolithic approaches. By isolating semantic failures from audit evidence from actual harm, the work enables researchers to design targeted mitigations—some addressing model reasoning, others focusing on logging and detection, still others on sandboxing and access control. The three-endpoint framework points toward compositional safety architectures where multiple layers each target a different failure mode. The benchmark also enables systematic study of which attack types remain effective against specific defense strategies, supporting evidence-based prioritization of safety investments in deployed systems. Future work can use SafeClawBench to evaluate emerging agent designs, multimodal systems, and more sophisticated attack strategies.
